text: This research investigates the proficiency of Large Language Models (LLMs), specifically GPT-2 and BERT, in representing geometric structures and their corresponding spatial relationships. By encoding geometries from Well-Known Text (WKT) formats into high-dimensional embeddings, the study evaluates the performance of these representations through various downstream tasks, including geometric attribute classification, numeric property regression, and spatial predicate prediction. The experimental results, derived from real-world geospatial datasets, indicate that while LLMs successfully identify geometry types and achieve up to 73% accuracy in predicting spatial predicates, they struggle significantly with precise numeric estimations such as area calculation, centroid derivation, and distance measurement. Furthermore, the models show limited capability in retrieving spatially related objects through textual descriptions. These findings suggest that while LLMs possess foundational knowledge of spatial syntax, current embedding methods—often hindered by coordinate fragmentation and tokenization loss—require the integration of domain-specific geographic knowledge and more sophisticated encoding strategies to effectively support advanced GeoAI applications.
